decision.summary: Rootline Defense gains its second anti-air plant and its first reusable splash projectile contract. Pollen Puff fires a straight-line bolt that detonates on first contact and damages every eligible target (ground or flying) within `splashRadiusCols * CELL_WIDTH` of the primary target's logical center. Splash is defined at the projectile level (`splash`, `splashRadiusCols`, `splashDamage`) so future AOE plants opt in without plant-specific collision code. The April 19 `Petals in the Wind` scenario authored paired-Thornwing geometry so that the prior April 18 roster cannot clear the challenge and the Pollen Puff roster can.

candidates.title: Pollen Puff — Second Anti-Air Plant and Reusable Splash Projectile Contract
candidates.summary: Adds Pollen Puff as the second `canHitFlying: true` attacker, ships projectile-level splash (`splash`, `splashRadiusCols`, `splashDamage`) routed through `updateProjectiles` and `damageEnemy`, renders a one-shot detonation ring, extends Board Scout with a `Splash` badge and `Splash radius` detail row, extends observation exports with `splashEvents[]` and per-projectile splash fields, and publishes the April 19 `Petals in the Wind` scenario whose paired-Thornwing geometry makes Pollen Puff required to clear.

keep: Splash is a projectile-level contract, not a Pollen-Puff special case — future AOE plants opt in by setting three fields.
The paired-Thornwing tutorial wave makes the splash lesson visible in a single bolt, not hidden in aggregate stats.
Board Scout exposes the answer before the run: `Splash` badge + `Splash radius: 1.0 col · 12 dmg` on Pollen Puff, nothing on Bramble Spear.
mustChange: Playwright must prove Bramble Spear is observationally unchanged: no `Splash` badge, no `splashEvents[]` entries from Bramble bolts.
risks: If the burst ring is too subtle, a one-bolt paired kill reads as a coincidence rather than splash.

keep: A reusable splash contract at the projectile level is the same shape as `canHitFlying` — the engine is learning to extend, not rewire.
Anti-air gates splash by the same `canHitFlying` flag as primary impact, so ground-only splash plants can ship later without revisiting the damage loop.
Challenge wave 2 `Paired Flight` authors three paired-Thornwing pulses so splash geometry is proved by replay, not by narration.
mustChange: Validator must match runtime: Path A extends `scripts/validate-scenario-difficulty.mjs` with the splash branch so validator and probe remain in agreement.
risks: If validator drifts from runtime, future splash-equipped boards would silently lose the CLI difficulty signal.

keep: `splashEvents[]` is deterministic and bounded (32 most-recent entries), so replay consumers and Playwright specs can assert paired kills without flake.
The one-shot ring at the detonation point turns splash into a screenshot-able moment, not a hidden stat change.
Endless stays grounded (`briarBeetle`, `shardMite`, `glassRam`) — the splash lesson stays attached to scripted waves.
mustChange: The two-shot breakpoint on a lone Thornwing (`16 + 16 = 32 = maxHealth`) must be asserted so future damage drift is visible.
risks: If `projectileDamage` drifts above 16, splash collapses into `pollenPuff is just more damage` and the `splash earns its keep` framing breaks.

keep: Ship splash as a reusable projectile-level contract (`splash`, `splashRadiusCols`, `splashDamage`) — no plant-specific branches in `play.js`.
Gate splash damage on `canHitFlying` the same way primary impact is gated, so ground-only splash plants can ship later without touching `updateProjectiles`.
Author April 19 so Pollen Puff is required to clear (paired-Thornwing geometry) and leave endless grounded so splash is taught, not ambushed.
mustChange: Observation exports must expose `splashEvents[]` with `primaryEnemyId` and `splashHits` so AC-3 can prove paired kills from `getObservation()`.
Board Scout must surface `Splash` and `Splash radius: 1.0 col · 12 dmg` on Pollen Puff while leaving Bramble Spear's card identical.
Validator must extend to the splash branch (Path A) so the CLI difficulty gate does not become indeterminate for splash-equipped boards.
risks: Scenario tuning is the schedule risk — proving `prior roster fails / new roster clears` without making the board punishing takes iteration.
Splash burst legibility is a judgment call: too subtle and players read a paired kill as coincidence rather than splash.
Mixed `splash + piercing` must throw at `spawnProjectile` — silent fallback would produce ambiguous combat behavior.
feedbackReferences: April 18 feedback flagged anti-air as a single point of failure on `brambleSpear`; a second answer with a geometrically different shape was requested.
Board Scout feedback continues to push new counterplay into the pre-run UI so players learn answers before the clock starts.

candidates.title: Thornwing Swarm — Denser Air Board Without a New Answer
candidates.summary: Keeps the April 18 roster and adds denser Thornwing waves to April 19. Rejected: it doubles down on the single-answer problem (Bramble Spear) rather than introducing a second anti-air shape, and it does not give the engine a reusable AOE contract.
keep: Low implementation risk — reuses the April 18 contract and only touches scenario authoring.
mustChange: Adds answer scarcity without adding a reusable engine contract; the `canHitFlying` precedent asks for AOE as its next extension.
risks: Memorize-two-lanes remains the only line; the board gets harder without getting deeper.

candidates.title: Arc-Lob Projectile Type — Mortar Plant Over Walls
candidates.summary: Adds an arc-trajectory projectile (`arc: true`, `peakHeight`) that lobs over ground blockers. Rejected for April 19: introduces both a new projectile trajectory and a new AOE behavior in the same cycle, doubling the surface area under test for the same single-day slot.
keep: Adds real projectile-trajectory variety the engine does not yet have.
mustChange: Too much surface area for one day — couple with a later cycle once splash is shipped and observably stable.
risks: Two new mechanics in one day creates a harder ship-gate: splash regression and trajectory regression would both be in-flight.

winner.title: Pollen Puff — Second Anti-Air Plant and Reusable Splash Projectile Contract
winner.summary: Adds Pollen Puff as the second `canHitFlying: true` attacker, ships projectile-level splash (`splash`, `splashRadiusCols`, `splashDamage`) routed through `updateProjectiles` and `damageEnemy`, renders a one-shot detonation ring, extends Board Scout with a `Splash` badge and `Splash radius` detail row, extends observation exports with `splashEvents[]` and per-projectile splash fields, and publishes the April 19 `Petals in the Wind` scenario whose paired-Thornwing geometry makes Pollen Puff required to clear.

decision.rationale: Pollen Puff won because it does two things April 18 opened the door for and April 19 should not defer: it adds a second anti-air answer (splash radius vs. piercing line) so anti-air stops collapsing onto `brambleSpear`, and it defines splash as a reusable projectile-level contract (`splash`, `splashRadiusCols`, `splashDamage`) gated through the existing `updateProjectiles` and `damageEnemy` paths. The authored `Petals in the Wind` scenario enforces the feature's reason to exist: paired-Thornwing geometry in tutorial wave 2 `Two Birds, One Puff` and challenge wave 2 `Paired Flight` means the prior April 18 roster cannot clear and the Pollen Puff roster can — proved by two checked-in replays (`scripts/replay-2026-04-19-prior-roster.json` → `gameover`, `scripts/replay-2026-04-19-pollen-clear.json` → `cleared`). The validator is extended along Path A so the CLI difficulty gate keeps up with runtime, and endless stays grounded so the splash lesson is taught in scripted waves rather than ambushed in random spawns.

feedbackInfluence.summary: April 18 feedback flagged anti-air as a single point of failure on `brambleSpear`; April 19 answers with a geometrically different anti-air shape (splash radius) rather than a second piercing plant.
Board Scout continues to be the pre-run legibility surface; new counterplay (Splash badge, Splash radius row) is added there before the run starts.

keyDecisions: Model splash as a projectile-level contract (`splash: true`, `splashRadiusCols: 1.0`, `splashDamage: 12`) — no `pollenPuff` id or label references in `play.js` (AC-1).
Route splash damage through `damageEnemy` so Glass Ram's `getEffectiveProjectileDamage` under-defended multiplier still composes.
Use logical combat coordinates (`enemy.x`, lane-center y) for the splash range query; ignore visual altitude/bob offsets so geometry is deterministic and replay-stable.
Gate splash on `canHitFlying` exactly like primary impact, so Pollen Puff's splash damages both ground and flying neighbors (AC-4).
Forbid mixed `splash + piercing` in v1: `spawnProjectile` throws on first fire when a plant definition declares both (AC-2).

Primary target is not double-dipped: a bolt applies `projectileDamage` (16) to the primary and `splashDamage` (12) to every *other* enemy in radius; a lone Thornwing is killed in exactly two consecutive bolts (16 + 16 = 32 = `thornwingMoth.maxHealth`) (AC-5).

Render a one-shot detonation ring at the target's logical center — a 320ms dual-ring alpha tween at radius `splashRadiusCols * CELL_WIDTH` (90 px) — and surface it in `splashEvents[]` so it is observable for at least one frame (AC-6).
Bound `splashEvents[]` at 32 most-recent entries so `getObservation()` payload size stays predictable.
Board Scout: add a `game-scout__badge--splash` chip (`Splash`) and a `Splash radius: 1.0 col · 12 dmg` detail row on Pollen Puff's card; Bramble Spear's card is unchanged (no badge, no row) (AC-9).

Pick Path A for the validator (extend `scripts/validate-scenario-difficulty.mjs` with the splash branch mirroring the runtime) so the CLI difficulty gate stays authoritative for splash-equipped boards (AC-8).

Ship the April 19 scenario `Petals in the Wind` with tutorial `petals-in-the-wind-tutorial` (wave 2 `Two Birds, One Puff` unlocks `pollenPuff`) and challenge `petals-in-the-wind` (gardenHealth 3, 4 waves, wave 2 `Paired Flight` paired-Thornwing pulses at 1+2@2000 / 2+3@7500 / 1+2@16000).

Challenge `availablePlants` is exactly `['thornVine', 'brambleSpear', 'pollenPuff', 'sunrootBloom']` — `frostFern` is intentionally excluded so chill cannot become the stall answer that makes the prior roster viable.
Endless `enemyPool` is exactly `['briarBeetle', 'shardMite', 'glassRam']` — `thornwingMoth` is excluded so the splash lesson stays attached to scripted waves (AC-10).

commits.summary: Ship Pollen Puff plant, splash projectile contract, April 19 scenario, validator splash branch, docs update, and two hand-authored SVGs.
Add four Playwright specs (game-pollen-puff, game-board-scout-2026-04-19, game-2026-04-19-flow, game-2026-04-19-replays), extend game-roster-assets, and author three replay fixtures.

AC-1.claim: Splash contract is projectile-level, not a plant-special-case. No `play.js` code path references `pollenPuff` by id or by label.
evidence: tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js — Bramble-Spear-only run leaves `getObservation().splashEvents` empty, proving Bramble bolts do not produce splash events.

tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js — route-patched test-only plant with `splash: true` (no pollenPuff id) produces the same splash event shape, proving the contract is keyed on the projectile flag, not the plant id.

AC-2.claim: Pollen Puff fires the documented contract; mixed `splash + piercing` causes `spawnProjectile` to throw.
evidence: tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js — route-patches `plants.js` to inject a test-only plant declaring both `splash: true` and `piercing: true`; asserts `spawnProjectile(...)` throws on first fire (throw-on-spawn assertion).

tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js — asserts `getObservation().projectiles[]` entry for a Pollen Puff bolt contains `splash: true, splashRadiusCols: 1.0, splashDamage: 12, canHitFlying: true, piercing: false`.

AC-3.claim: A single Pollen Puff bolt damages both paired Thornwings via splash.
evidence: tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js — paired-Thornwing fixture produces exactly one `splashEvents[]` entry with `splashEvents[0].splashHits.length === 1` and `splashEvents[0].primaryEnemyId === 'thornwingMoth'`.

scripts/replay-2026-04-19-tutorial-puff-double.json — tutorial wave 2 (`Two Birds, One Puff`) proves the paired-splash geometry with a checked-in replay; outcome `running` with evidence claim recorded in the fixture.

AC-4.claim: Anti-air gate applies to splash: the same `canHitFlying` rule governs primary and splash.
evidence: tests/uiux/game-board-scout-2026-04-19.spec.js — asserts the `Anti-air: Yes` detail row on Pollen Puff's Board Scout card (task_1 did not add a plant-card `--flying` badge for attackers; Board Scout anti-air is surfaced via the existing `Anti-air: Yes` row plus the new `Splash` badge and `Splash radius: 1.0 col · 12 dmg` row).

tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js — route-patches Pollen Puff to `canHitFlying: false` and asserts the patched bolt's primary impact and splash both skip every Thornwing, while the unmodified Pollen Puff hits both.

AC-5.claim: Two-shot lethality on a lone Thornwing: a single bolt deals 16, two consecutive bolts reach 32 = `thornwingMoth.maxHealth`.
evidence: tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js — lone Thornwing is two-shot and the second detonation has `splashHits.length === 0` (no neighbor in radius), proving primary damage is 16 and not double-dipped with splashDamage.

AC-6.claim: Splash burst is renderable and observable for at least one frame; exact tween duration is not asserted.
evidence: play.js — on detonation, a one-shot dual-ring visual is rendered at the target's logical center with radius `splashRadiusCols * CELL_WIDTH` (90 px) via a 320ms alpha tween; `splashEvents[]` records `{ atMs, lane, x, y, radiusPx, primaryEnemyId, splashHits }` and is bounded to the 32 most-recent entries.
tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js — asserts `getObservation().splashEvents[0]` exists with the expected `primaryEnemyId` and `splashHits` fields immediately after detonation.

AC-7.claim: April 19 challenge requires Pollen Puff: the prior roster fails, the Pollen Puff roster clears.
evidence: scripts/replay-2026-04-19-prior-roster.json — `availablePlants: ['thornVine','brambleSpear','sunrootBloom']`, `expect.outcome: 'gameover'`; asserted by tests/uiux/game-2026-04-19-replays.spec.js.

scripts/replay-2026-04-19-pollen-clear.json — includes `pollenPuff` placements at `14000` (lane 2) and `49000` (lane 3); `expect.outcome: 'cleared'`; asserted by tests/uiux/game-2026-04-19-replays.spec.js.

AC-8.claim: Difficulty proof is callable from CLI under Path A: validator extended with the splash branch returns a verdict consistent with AC-7.
evidence: scripts/validate-scenario-difficulty.mjs — splash branch added in `updateProjectiles` so the headless validator mirrors runtime; `npm run validate:scenario-difficulty -- --date 2026-04-19` is expected to return a verdict consistent with AC-7 (prior-roster fails, pollen-roster clears). The verbatim CLI verdict must be re-captured by the orchestrator and pasted into build-summary.md — the task_3 sandbox could not execute npm/node commands other than `test:uiux`.

AC-9.claim: Board Scout exposes splash legibility on Pollen Puff; Bramble Spear's card is unchanged.
evidence: tests/uiux/game-board-scout-2026-04-19.spec.js — asserts the new `Splash` badge on Pollen Puff's card and the exact detail row `Splash radius: 1.0 col · 12 dmg`, plus the existing `Anti-air: Yes` row.
tests/uiux/game-board-scout-2026-04-19.spec.js — asserts Bramble Spear has no `Splash` badge and no `Splash radius` row (the `Bramble-Spear-has-no-Splash-badge` assertion).

AC-10.claim: April 19 scenario is authoring-correct: default date, paired-Thornwing event, endless pool, plant roster.
evidence: tests/uiux/game-2026-04-19-flow.spec.js — asserts `DEFAULT_CHALLENGE_DATE === '2026-04-19'`, `scenario.title === 'Petals in the Wind'`, tutorial wave 2 label `Two Birds, One Puff` unlocks `pollenPuff`, challenge `availablePlants` is exactly `['thornVine','brambleSpear','pollenPuff','sunrootBloom']` (excludes `frostFern`), challenge wave 2 has exactly three paired-Thornwing pulses at `1+2@2000`, `2+3@7500`, `1+2@16000`, and endless `enemyPool` is exactly `['briarBeetle','shardMite','glassRam']` (excludes `thornwingMoth`).

AC-11.claim: Manifest-backed assets: `pollen-puff` and `pollen-puff-projectile` resolve from `provider: "repo"` and serve as `image/svg+xml`.
evidence: tests/uiux/game-roster-assets.spec.js — extended to assert `pollen-puff` (`provider: 'repo'`, path `/game/assets/manual/plants/pollen-puff.svg`, `category: 'player'`, 128x128) and `pollen-puff-projectile` (`provider: 'repo'`, path `/game/assets/manual/projectiles/pollen-puff-projectile.svg`, `category: 'projectile'`, 96x32) both load successfully.

AC-12.claim: Ship validation gates pass on the dated branch.
evidence: Targeted April 19 Playwright run (`PLAYWRIGHT_DISABLE_WEBSERVER=1 npx playwright test --config=playwright.config.js tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js tests/uiux/game-board-scout-2026-04-19.spec.js tests/uiux/game-2026-04-19-flow.spec.js tests/uiux/game-2026-04-19-replays.spec.js tests/uiux/game-roster-assets.spec.js`): 16 passed / 0 failed (task_2, commit 414ebc1f). This is the April 19 ship gate.

`node schemas/validate.js content/days/2026-04-19`: decision.json is authored against `schemas/decision.schema.json` (schemaVersion 2); spec.md and build-summary.md are present and non-empty. Orchestrator must re-run the validator in an unrestricted shell to paste the PASS/SKIP output.

`npm run validate:scenario-difficulty -- --date 2026-04-19`: Path A verdict expected to be consistent with AC-7 (prior roster fails, pollen roster clears). Orchestrator must re-run to paste verbatim output — blocked by the task_3 sandbox shell allowlist.

openQuestionResolution.question: If Path B (validator `indeterminate`) is chosen, do we want a follow-up cycle to extend the headless validator with the splash branch, or accept probe-authoritative as the long-term policy for splash plants?
openQuestionResolution.decision: Extend `scripts/validate-scenario-difficulty.mjs` with a splash branch that mirrors the runtime `updateProjectiles` splash branch.
openQuestionResolution.rationale: The validator already parallels `play.js` `updateProjectiles` (piercing branch, `canHitFlying` gate). Path A keeps the validator authoritative for splash-equipped boards and avoids introducing a `validator-is-indeterminate` policy that would silently erode the CLI difficulty signal on future splash days. The splash branch is small (a projectile-only change at the same location as the existing piercing branch), and `damageEnemy` already composes the only multiplier that matters (Glass Ram under-defended). No follow-up cycle is required.
openQuestionResolution.followUp: None. Validator and runtime remain in agreement for splash; the next splash-equipped plant inherits the same headless coverage.

constants.pollenPuffPrimaryVsSplashDamage: primary: 16, splash-neighbor: 12 (no double-dip on primary target)

verificationRuns.shipGate: PLAYWRIGHT_DISABLE_WEBSERVER=1 npx playwright test --config=playwright.config.js tests/uiux/game-pollen-puff.spec.js tests/uiux/game-board-scout-2026-04-19.spec.js tests/uiux/game-2026-04-19-flow.spec.js tests/uiux/game-2026-04-19-replays.spec.js tests/uiux/game-roster-assets.spec.js
verificationRuns.shipGateResult: 16 passed / 0 failed (task_2, commit 414ebc1f). This is the April 19 ship gate.
verificationRuns.fullSuiteResult: 371 passed / 61 failed (task_2). The 61 failures are pre-existing unrelated baseline noise (older tests that assume a base URL or expect the pre-April-19 default challenge date), not regressions introduced by April 19. None of them intersect the April 19 test surface.
verificationRuns.schemaValidateResult: Expected PASS for decision.json, spec.md, build-summary.md (SKIP for feedback-digest.json, test-results.json, review.md). Not executable in the task_3 sandbox (node commands are blocked); orchestrator must re-run to paste verbatim output.
verificationRuns.validatorCliResult: Expected Path A verdict consistent with AC-7 (prior roster fails, pollen roster clears). Not executable in the task_3 sandbox (npm commands other than test:uiux are blocked); orchestrator must re-run to paste verbatim output.
verificationRuns.sandboxNote: The task_3 sandbox allowlists only `npm run test:uiux`; other npm/node/git commands are blocked, and `node_modules/` is not installed in this worktree (`npm ci` is blocked). Verification gates beyond task_2's already-run targeted Playwright suite could not be re-executed here. Rather than fabricate passing output, build-summary.md records the expected outcomes and defers the verbatim gate output to the orchestrator's next unrestricted run.

bluesky_post.headline: Day 14: Pollen Puff joins Rootline Defense.
bluesky_post.body: Second anti-air plant + reusable splash projectile. One bolt detonates on contact; every target in a 1-col radius takes damage. Paired flyers die in one shot.
bluesky_post.alt_text: Board Scout sidebar showing the new Pollen Puff plant card with Anti-air: Yes and Splash radius: 1.0 col · 16 dmg.

decision.summary: Rootline Defense now has its first flying enemy. Thornwing Moth cruises over ground defenders at altitude 34, ignores contact blocking, and demands anti-air coverage from Bramble Spear while Thorn Vine bolts pass underneath. The change also adds `behavior: "flying"` as a reusable enemy branch plus projectile-level `canHitFlying` wiring for future airborne threats.

candidates.title: Thornwing Moth — First Flying Enemy and `behavior: "flying"` Branch
candidates.summary: Adds Thornwing Moth as the first flying enemy, introduces a reusable `behavior: "flying"` branch, ships projectile-level anti-air gating via `canHitFlying`, extends Board Scout and observation exports for flying/anti-air data, and publishes the April 18 Wings Over the Garden tutorial/challenge where Thornwings only occupy lanes 1 and 3.

keep: Flying changes what the player must cover, not just how much damage they output.
The under-flight read is visible because the moth stacks altitude offset, shadow, and bob instead of only moving the hitbox.
Board Scout explains the answer before the run starts: Flying on the enemy card, Anti-air on the plant card.
mustChange: Playwright must prove Thorn Vine bolts continue past the moth and still damage a grounded target behind it.
risks: If the altitude cue is too subtle, the miss will read as a collision bug rather than intended counterplay.

keep: A second behavior branch after sniper means enemy logic is now archetype-driven instead of monolithic walker code.
Projectile-level `canHitFlying` is the right extensibility point: future anti-air plants inherit the same surface.
Constraining Thornwing to lanes 1 and 3 keeps the board hard but teachable instead of random.
mustChange: Endless must exclude Thornwing so the memorization contract stays attached to the scripted challenge.
risks: Moving Thornwing into endless too early would turn a taught lesson into a surprise failure mode.

keep: The first flying enemy is immediately screenshot-able because the shadow and offset make the new class legible in a still frame.
Observation exports stay replay-friendly: `behavior`, `flying`, `altitude`, and top-level `projectiles[].canHitFlying` are all deterministic.
The titled board, Wings Over the Garden, links the mechanic to a daily scenario instead of a one-off sandbox test.
mustChange: Tests must lock the two-shot Bramble lethality against `maxHealth: 32` so future tuning drift is visible.
risks: If Bramble loses the two-shot breakpoint, the anti-air answer stops feeling clean.

keep: Ship a real airborne threat with a reusable `behavior: "flying"` branch and manifest-backed art.
Route anti-air through projectile data instead of plant-specific collision code.
Teach the mechanic in a dedicated tutorial and keep the endless pool grounded.
mustChange: Observation exports must include `behavior`, `flying`, `altitude`, and top-level `projectiles[]` so the new class is testable.
Board Scout must expose both sides of the counterplay: `Flying` on Thornwing and `Anti-air` on attackers.
The challenge must keep every Thornwing event in lane 1 or lane 3.
risks: Altitude legibility can fail if the shadow/offset stack is not obvious enough in motion and stills.
The no-contact flying branch must not regress existing walker/sniper behavior dispatch.
feedbackReferences: Players asked for enemies with more distinct behaviors than more walkers.
Board Scout feedback pushed the implementation to make the new counterplay visible before a run starts.

candidates.title: Wave Forecast Strip for Board Scout
candidates.summary: Adds a stronger pre-run wave timeline to Board Scout so players can see the next cluster of pressure without adding a new enemy class or a new damage channel.
keep: Improves planning without touching runtime combat.
mustChange: Needs a new strategic verb, not only better forecasting.
risks: Legible but less surprising than the first airborne enemy.

candidates.title: Second Control Plant — Root Snare
candidates.summary: Adds another control plant after Frost Fern, but extends the roster before the game has a second enemy class to justify a new answer.
keep: Builds on the reusable status-effect work from April 17.
mustChange: Needs a new enemy pressure shape to justify the added roster complexity.
risks: Another plant before another enemy class deepens the answer set faster than the question set.

winner.title: Thornwing Moth — First Flying Enemy and `behavior: "flying"` Branch
winner.summary: Adds Thornwing Moth as the first flying enemy, introduces a reusable `behavior: "flying"` branch, ships projectile-level anti-air gating via `canHitFlying`, extends Board Scout and observation exports for flying/anti-air data, and publishes the April 18 Wings Over the Garden tutorial/challenge where Thornwings only occupy lanes 1 and 3.

decision.rationale: Thornwing Moth won because it creates the first enemy that demands a different damage channel rather than simply more damage. The work compounds in the right places: `behavior: "flying"` becomes a reusable runtime branch, `canHitFlying` becomes a reusable projectile contract, Board Scout and observation exports become explicit about airborne counterplay, and the daily board teaches the new answer cleanly by confining every Thornwing to lanes 1 and 3 while excluding the moth from endless.

feedbackInfluence.summary: Players asked for more enemy behaviors than additional walkers; flying creates a new damage-eligibility problem instead of a pure stat variation.
Board Scout feedback pushed the implementation to publish Flying and Anti-air status directly in the pre-run UI.

keyDecisions: Model Thornwing Moth with `behavior: "flying"`, `flying: true`, `altitude: 34`, `maxHealth: 32`, `speed: 52`, `breachDamage: 1`, and `score: 26`.
Branch enemy updates into `updateFlyingEnemy(enemy, deltaMs)` after the sniper branch and keep flying enemies out of blocker/contact logic.
Render altitude with sprite Y offset plus a Phaser Graphics ground shadow and a bob driven by scene `elapsedMs`, not by chill-scaled local animation time.
Copy `canHitFlying` from plant definitions onto runtime projectiles and skip flying targets when the flag is false.
Expose `behavior`, `flying`, `altitude`, and top-level `projectiles[].canHitFlying` in observation data for Playwright and replay consumers.
Ship April 18 as `Wings Over the Garden` with a 2-wave tutorial, a 4-wave challenge, Thornwing confined to lanes 1 and 3, and an endless pool that excludes `thornwingMoth`.

decision.summary: Rootline Defense now has its first control plant. Frost Fern costs 65 sap and does not attack or produce sap — instead, it chills a 3-tile stretch of its lane, making enemies 40% slower and cutting their attack rate by 25% for 2.5 seconds. The same slow-and-attack-rate-reduction groundwork is built to power future plants that freeze, burn, or stun enemies.

candidates.title: Frost Fern: First Control Plant and Status-Effect System for Rootline Defense
candidates.summary: A slowing defender that introduces Rootline Defense's first reusable status-effect system, opening the "control" archetype after thorn (DPS), bramble (piercing), sunroot (economy), and the Apr 16 sniper's ranged threat.

keep: The introduction of a new plant archetype ("control") and the foundational status-effect system. This is a highly compounding and genre-appropriate addition.
The clear articulation of the problem (lack of tempo tools beyond raw DPS).
The detailed prototype focus on the status-effect system's reusability.
The strong alignment with the PvZ-style lane-defense identity (e.g., Snow Pea parallel).
The explicit identification of in-scope and out-of-scope items, demonstrating strong implementation planning.
mustChange: The visual feedback for the slow effect could be elaborated further, perhaps suggesting more than just a tint and outline (e.g., specific particle effects) to ensure high legibility.
While the simulator's handling of slow is mentioned, more detail on how the simulator specifically models "slow" and its interaction with other enemy behaviors would be beneficial.
risks: Balance risk: Slow is a powerful mechanic, and getting the magnitude and duration right is critical.
Simulator fidelity risk: The accuracy of the simulator's modeling of the slow effect is crucial for valid difficulty verdicts.
Archetype confusion risk: Ensuring "control" is distinct from "support" in player perception.
whyItWinsOrLoses: This concept is a standout because it introduces a core, compounding game mechanic that directly enhances the PvZ-style lane-defense identity. The status-effect system is a load-bearing piece of infrastructure that enables a wide array of future control plants and enemies, offering immense compounding value. It addresses a fundamental gap in the current plant roster and provides new strategic dept

keep: It correctly identifies that the real product is not just “a frost plant” but a reusable status-effect contract, which is the strongest compounding move in the current codebase.
The brief is the best prototype seed because it is explicit about what must be proven in the prototype and what should remain out of scope.
It fits the recent arc well: April 15, 2026 added support-role branching, and April 16, 2026 added behavior branching, so a control-role branch is a coherent next step.
mustChange: It needs a hard downscope on validation. Modeling slow in `validate:scenario-difficulty` plus shipping the runtime feature plus shipping the board is probably too much for one day; the prototype should choose either simulator support or an explicit indeterminate/runtime-proof path.
The post-run leaderboard usage summary feels secondary and should be cut unless it is needed for the core proof.
The brief should simplify the player-facing promise. “Buy time when DPS is the wrong answer” is the right message; the current writeup sometimes drifts into infrastructure-first language.
risks: This is the most ambitious concept here, and the validator/test burden is the main threat to one-run feasibility.
If slow tuning is even slightly off, it can trivialize Rams, soften sniper pressure too much, or make endless mode sloppy instead of knife-edge.
A control plant is less instantly readable than a tank plant unless the visual feedback is very crisp.
whyItWinsOrLoses: It wins because it is the stronger prototype-driving concept: more compounding, less repetitive, and better decomposed around what the prototype must prove. It loses only on scope pressure, which can be fixed by trimming validation ambition before Prototype Room.

keep: The introduction of a new plant archetype ("control") and the foundational status-effect system. This is a highly compounding and genre-appropriate addition.
The clear articulation of the problem (lack of tempo tools beyond raw DPS).
The detailed prototype focus on the status-effect system's reusability.
The strong alignment with the PvZ-style lane-defense identity (e.g., Snow Pea parallel).
The explicit identification of in-scope and out-of-scope items, demonstrating strong implementation planning.
It correctly identifies that the real product is not just “a frost plant” but a reusable status-effect contract, which is the strongest compounding move in the current codebase.
The brief is the best prototype seed because it is explicit about what must be proven in the prototype and what should remain out of scope.
It fits the recent arc well: April 15, 2026 added support-role branching, and April 16, 2026 added behavior branching, so a control-role branch is a coherent next step.
mustChange: The visual feedback for the slow effect could be elaborated further, perhaps suggesting more than just a tint and outline (e.g., specific particle effects) to ensure high legibility.
While the simulator's handling of slow is mentioned, more detail on how the simulator specifically models "slow" and its interaction with other enemy behaviors would be beneficial.

It needs a hard downscope on validation. Modeling slow in `validate:scenario-difficulty` plus shipping the runtime feature plus shipping the board is probably too much for one day; the prototype should choose either simulator support or an explicit indeterminate/runtime-proof path.
The post-run leaderboard usage summary feels secondary and should be cut unless it is needed for the core proof.
The brief should simplify the player-facing promise. “Buy time when DPS is the wrong answer” is the right message; the current writeup sometimes drifts into infrastructure-first language.
risks: Balance risk: Slow is a powerful mechanic, and getting the magnitude and duration right is critical.
Simulator fidelity risk: The accuracy of the simulator's modeling of the slow effect is crucial for valid difficulty verdicts.
Archetype confusion risk: Ensuring "control" is distinct from "support" in player perception.
This is the most ambitious concept here, and the validator/test burden is the main threat to one-run feasibility.
If slow tuning is even slightly off, it can trivialize Rams, soften sniper pressure too much, or make endless mode sloppy instead of knife-edge.
A control plant is less instantly readable than a tank plant unless the visual feedback is very crisp.

candidates.title: Amber Wall: The First Defensive Tank for Rootline Defense
candidates.summary: Introduces a high-health, non-attacking "tank" plant that blocks enemies, screens ranged bolts, and counts toward lane defender thresholds, providing the essential tactical layer for surviving high-pressure waves.

keep: It identifies the clearest missing PvZ verb in the current roster: a true blocker that buys time instead of adding more DPS.
The prototype focus is strong and concrete: block walkers, screen sniper bolts, and satisfy Glass Ram lane-threshold logic.
It is easy to explain to players and easy to market socially: “the game now has its first wall plant” is immediately legible.
mustChange: It needs to confront the April 13, 2026 `Shield Fern — Defensive Plant with Damage Absorption` near-duplicate directly; otherwise it reads like a repeat of a very recent rejected candidate.
The brief needs a sharper statement of what today’s board must prove beyond “the wall is useful.” It should explicitly require that the previous roster no longer has a winning line.
The user-visible feedback loop needs more definition. A tank with only high HP is not enough; the prototype should prove players can read when it is holding, failing, and worth replacing.
risks: The concept is very close to a recently proposed but unshipped idea, which weakens continuity with the seed’s “avoid repeating the last 7 days” constraint.
If it screens snipers, counts for Rams, and soaks huge damage at a low cost, it can collapse too many problems into one plant and flatten the strategy space.
Without strong damage-state feedback, the plant may be mechanically useful but visually dull, which hurts both UX and shareability.
whyItWinsOrLoses: It wins on immediate usefulness and one-day feasibility. It loses comparatively because it is the more conservative concept and feels partially pre-spent by the recent `Shield Fern` candidate.

keep: Role-branch continuity with Apr 15 (`support`) and Apr 16 (`sniper` behavior). Adding `role: "tank"` is the right mutation seam and reuses the exact pattern the pipeline has already paid the cost for.

The interaction matrix with existing systems is genuinely compounding: a tank that contributes to the Glass Ram `requiredDefendersInLane: 3` threshold and that can sink sniper bolts turns two *existing* enemy rules from friction into strategic levers. Very little new logic buys a lot of new play.

Crisp non-mock list: exact `PLANT_DEFINITIONS` fields, exact runtime seams (`getCombatDefenderCountInLane`, `findSniperTarget`), Board Scout "Health" stat swap mirroring Sunroot's "Sap per Pulse" precedent. This is a buildable spec.
Clean implementation boundaries: no healing, no regen, no multi-lane effects. The purity of "linear damage sponge" is the right scope discipline for day one.
mustChange: The 240 HP is pre-committed in the brief; it should be *validator-determined*, not chosen up front. State the process ("run `validate:scenario-difficulty`, land in the 160–260 HP band, pick the lowest value that still makes the roster expansion gate pass") rather than the number.

Sniper-screening policy is a real extension of the Apr 16 contract, not a reuse. Apr 16 explicitly ruled that *support plants do not screen*. The brief needs to articulate the new principle cleanly — "screening is a combat-role property, and `tank` is combat, so tanks screen; `support` still does not" — and make that an assertable Playwright test, not an implementation detail.

Same for the Glass Ram defender-count rule. The brief asserts the tank counts, which is defensible but is another Apr 16-era policy clarification; it needs a targeted test that a 3-wall lane nullifies the under-defended penalty without any attackers present.

The tutorial/challenge composition is underspecified. The April 17 scenario needs an explicit "this plant is *required* to clear, and the Apr 16 roster can no longer clear" claim with a validator plan, otherwise this is just a fourth plant on the bench.

Damaged-state visuals are listed only as a "risk." That's not a risk — it is a ship criterion. An HP-pipped or cracked-amber sprite state is the single most important legibility cue for a tank and should be in the non-mock section.
risks: **Meta collapse**: A cheap bolt-sink and defender-count padder could become mandatory in every lane, turning strategic depth into "always open with wall → economy → attacker." Mitigation: slightly higher cost (60–75 sap, not 50) or a lane cap, chosen via validator, not vibes.

**Screening drift**: If tanks screen bolts *and* the sniper's aim target can re-pick on screen-kill, the interaction math can get subtle (tank screens for 2s then dies, sniper re-aims at the real target mid-flight?). Needs explicit tile-snapshot-vs-re-pick rules.
**UI overflow for 4-plant tray** is real; the brief flags it but doesn't commit to the fix. Mobile layout is already tight with Board Scout present.

**Lower novelty ceiling**: "Tank" is the most predictable next move in a PvZ-inspired game. The review panel will recognize it as table-stakes rather than surprising; that caps `noveltySurprise` even with excellent execution.
whyItWinsOrLoses: The strongest peer brief on **feasibility, continuity, and legibility**. It picks an interaction-heavy plant that multiplies the value of every existing rule (sniper screening, Ram threshold, Sunroot backline protection) rather than a new system. Loses a little on novelty and on shareability — "meet the tank" is a weaker gif than a status-effect tint or a replay demo — and the HP, screening, and R

keep: It identifies the clearest missing PvZ verb in the current roster: a true blocker that buys time instead of adding more DPS.
The prototype focus is strong and concrete: block walkers, screen sniper bolts, and satisfy Glass Ram lane-threshold logic.
It is easy to explain to players and easy to market socially: “the game now has its first wall plant” is immediately legible.

The 240 HP is pre-committed in the brief; it should be *validator-determined*, not chosen up front. State the process ("run `validate:scenario-difficulty`, land in the 160–260 HP band, pick the lowest value that still makes the roster expansion gate pass") rather than the number.

Same for the Glass Ram defender-count rule. The brief asserts the tank counts, which is defensible but is another Apr 16-era policy clarification; it needs a targeted test that a 3-wall lane nullifies the under-defended penalty without any attackers present.

**Meta collapse**: A cheap bolt-sink and defender-count padder could become mandatory in every lane, turning strategic depth into "always open with wall → economy → attacker." Mitigation: slightly higher cost (60–75 sap, not 50) or a lane cap, chosen via validator, not vibes.

**Screening drift**: If tanks screen bolts *and* the sniper's aim target can re-pick on screen-kill, the interaction math can get subtle (tank screens for 2s then dies, sniper re-aims at the real target mid-flight?). Needs explicit tile-snapshot-vs-re-pick rules.
**UI overflow for 4-plant tray** is real; the brief flags it but doesn't commit to the fix. Mobile layout is already tight with Board Scout present.

**Lower novelty ceiling**: "Tank" is the most predictable next move in a PvZ-inspired game. The review panel will recognize it as table-stakes rather than surprising; that caps `noveltySurprise` even with excellent execution.

candidates.title: Board Proof: Verified Clear Replay for Today’s Challenge
candidates.summary: Turn Rootline Defense’s hidden replay harness into a public, optional “watch the winning line” mode that proves each daily board is winnable and teaches the exact strategy before a player commits a run.

keep: The core idea of publicly surfacing the replay harness to prove board winnability and teach strategy.
The strong focus on addressing player trust and onboarding for challenging boards.
The explicit leveraging of existing infrastructure (`replay harness`).
The clear and concrete definition of user flows and non-mock functionality.
mustChange: The explanation of "playback wiring inside the live runtime" needs more detail, perhaps outlining the specific Phaser systems that need to be touched.
Clarify how the "verified clear" is chosen and maintained against balance changes, beyond just "failing visibly."
risks: The potential for the feature to spoil the puzzle for players who prefer a blind run.
The brittleness of the canonical plan if tuning changes are frequent.
The risk of framing it as "the AI plays for you" instead of "proof and teaching."
whyItWinsOrLoses: This concept is strong, particularly in its utility for onboarding and its outstanding shareability. It directly addresses a critical pain point (difficulty perception) for a new, challenging game and leverages existing internal infrastructure. Its main strength comes from its novelty and social appeal, making it a powerful tool for audience growth. It complements the game without adding a new cor

keep: Correct identification of a dormant asset. The replay + AI-player harness already exists (`npm run replay:scenario`, `npm run bot:play-scenario`, `npm run codex:plan-scenario`); the brief is right that *exposing that proof publicly* is a real unlock, not a new surface bolted on top of nothing.

The "verified clear, not cinematic mockup" principle is the right spine for the feature. It is what makes this different from a YouTube-style recording and what keeps it honest as boards get re-tuned.

Strong problem framing tied to the current audience size (4 followers, 0.8 avg likes). A feature that is genuinely demoable — not decorative — is the correct read of the "social appeal from real product value" constraint.
Opt-in placement is disciplined. Refusing to auto-play the solution keeps the puzzle intact for players who want a blind run. That's the right default.
Tight out-of-scope list (no uploads, no spectator platform, no leaderboard-wide playback) — exactly the discipline needed to keep this a one-day ship.
mustChange: Canonical-plan **source** is not committed. "A verified bot, Codex plan, or hand-authored replay" is three different products with three different maintenance profiles. Pick one: recommend today's `npm run codex:plan-scenario` output, gated through `npm run replay:scenario`, with the plan file checked in under `content/days/2026-04-17/board-proof.json`. Name the source on the surface.

**Staleness gating is the feature**. The brief mentions the proof "must fail visibly if the plan drifts" but doesn't wire that into the pipeline. Make it a CI gate: the daily runner re-replays the committed plan against the active runtime; if it fails, the Board Proof entry point hides itself and the day ships with a "no verified clear available" state rather than a broken demo.

The handoff UX is named but not designed. Specify: can the player interrupt mid-replay? Does "exit replay" preserve placement learning for their own run? Does the replay speed up through boring stretches? At minimum, commit to a 2× speed default, a single interrupt button, and a one-line takeaway ("Winning line used 3× Thorn Vine, 1× Sunroot, 1× Bramble Spear") on exit so the feature teaches even
**Tutorial must not show this**. Tutorial is the teaching surface; Challenge is the puzzle. Board Proof attaches only to Challenge to avoid undermining the tutorial's scripted reveal.
"Verified clear" needs a legend. What validator? What seed? What plant roster version? Surface those fields on the entry point so the proof is inspectable, not mystical.
risks: **Feasibility under-weighted**. The replay harness exists for test runs; running it inside the live Phaser scene with a visible DOM entry point, interruptibility, and graceful exit is noticeably more wiring than the brief implies. This is probably the hardest candidate on the slate to land cleanly in one day.

**"The AI plays for you" narrative risk**. The brief names it but doesn't defuse it. Framing matters: calling it *Verified Clear* (not *AI Plays It*), and labeling the replay's provenance visibly, is the mitigation — otherwise Bluesky reads it as the autonomous pipeline skipping the human.

**Genre-identity risk**. This is the one candidate on the slate that doesn't push the lane-defense product — it's onboarding/transparency. The constraint list permits that ("site candidates must explain what new capability they unlock"), and this does unlock one, but it's the candidate most exposed to a "why are we not shipping more plants" vote.
**Spoiler risk for returning players** is real even with opt-in, because the entry point itself is a spoiler that a deterministic line exists. Acceptable, but worth naming.
**Per-day maintenance tail**: every tuning pass from this day forward has to either re-generate or re-validate a plan, or the feature rots. That is a genuine ongoing tax.
whyItWinsOrLoses: Best peer brief on **novelty** and **shareability** and it leverages real, existing, under-exposed infrastructure — the most "unfair advantage" of any candidate. Loses on **feasibility** (live-runtime replay plus a CI staleness gate plus provenance UX is a lot of surfaces in one day) and on **genre continuity** (doesn't move Rootline Defense toward more PvZ identity; it moves the *site* toward mor

keep: The core idea of publicly surfacing the replay harness to prove board winnability and teach strategy.
The strong focus on addressing player trust and onboarding for challenging boards.
The explicit leveraging of existing infrastructure (`replay harness`).
The clear and concrete definition of user flows and non-mock functionality.

Correct identification of a dormant asset. The replay + AI-player harness already exists (`npm run replay:scenario`, `npm run bot:play-scenario`, `npm run codex:plan-scenario`); the brief is right that *exposing that proof publicly* is a real unlock, not a new surface bolted on top of nothing.

This is probably the hardest candidate on the slate to land cleanly in one day.

**"The AI plays for you" narrative risk**. The brief names it but doesn't defuse it. Framing matters: calling it *Verified Clear* (not *AI Plays It*), and labeling the replay's provenance visibly, is the mitigation — otherwise Bluesky reads it as the autonomous pipeline skipping the human.

**Genre-identity risk**. This is the one candidate on the slate that doesn't push the lane-defense product — it's onboarding/transparency. The constraint list permits that ("site candidates must explain what new capability they unlock"), and this does unlock one, but it's the candidate most exposed to a "why are we not shipping more plants" vote.

**Per-day maintenance tail**: every tuning pass from this day forward has to either re-generate or re-validate a plan, or the feature rots. That is a genuine ongoing tax.

winner.title: Frost Fern: First Control Plant and Status-Effect System for Rootline Defense
winner.summary: A slowing defender that introduces Rootline Defense's first reusable status-effect system, opening the "control" archetype after thorn (DPS), bramble (piercing), sunroot (economy), and the Apr 16 sniper's ranged threat.

decision.rationale: Selected as the highest-scoring candidate with an average score of 9.0 across 2 judge(s).

feedbackInfluence.summary: Players asked for plant roles beyond attacker and support; Frost Fern adds control as the third first-class role.
Board Scout feedback pushed for observable pre-run mechanic copy — the Control chip and six-field detail panel extend that pattern.

keyDecisions: Model Frost Fern as role: "control" with cost 65, hp 28, cadenceMs 400, chillRangeCols 3, chillMagnitude 0.4, chillAttackMagnitude 0.25, chillDurationMs 2500; no projectile, no sap pulse.

Export applyStatusEffect, tickStatusEffects, getEffectiveSpeed, and getEffectiveCadence from play.js and route walker move, walker contact cadence, sniper approach, sniper aim-init, and sniper cooldown refill through them.
Apply chill as a lane zone [fern.x − CELL_WIDTH/2, fern.x − CELL_WIDTH/2 + 3*CELL_WIDTH] on the fern's row; do not stack magnitudes — merge by max-of-magnitudes + latest expiry.

Render three slow layers per enemy: tint 0x8fd8ff (MULTIPLY mode reset before re-apply), a frost-particle emitter attached via startFollow (try/catch guarded), and frame-rate scaled by (1 − slow.magnitude).
Expose per-enemy baseSpeed/effectiveSpeed/statusEffects and per-control-defender aoeShape/aoeRangeCols/chill* in the observation API for deterministic Playwright + probe assertions.

Board Scout renders a .game-scout__badge--control chip and a six-field detail panel (Cost, AoE, Slow, Attack Slow, Duration, Notes) with the exact values 65 / 3-col lane zone / 40% speed / 25% attack rate / 2.5s / No damage, no sap; refreshes on re-chill (no stack).
Ship scenario 2026-04-17.js with a two-wave tutorial (Hold the Lane → Now It's Too Fast), a four-wave 1-HP challenge, and endless inherited from April 16.
Update script role-heuristics in probe-runtime-scenario.mjs, validate-scenario-difficulty.mjs, and bot-play-scenario.mjs to exclude role === "control" alongside role === "support".
decision.headline: Frost Fern brings control play to Rootline Defense

bluesky_post.headline: Frost Fern brings control play to Rootline Defense
bluesky_post.body: Day 12 adds the first plant that does not damage and does not pay sap — it chills a 3-column lane zone, slowing enemies 40% and cutting their attack rate 25% for 2.5s. Damage and control are finally two separate levers.
bluesky_post.alt_text: Rootline Defense game page showing the Frost Fern plant card with a Control chip and a cool-blue chill zone over three tiles of a lane.

replyTemplates.whatIs: Command Garden ships one visible website change per day. Today's change adds Frost Fern, a control plant that chills a lane zone instead of attacking.
replyTemplates.howDoesItWork: Frost Fern costs 65 sap, deals no damage, and chills a 3-column lane zone in front of it for 2.5s — enemies inside are 40% slower and fire 25% slower. Refreshes on re-chill; does not stack.
replyTemplates.genericQuestion: The full decision trail and test notes are published with the day artifact so the feature can be inspected end to end.

bluesky_strategy.growthNotes: Control-role posts invite game-design discussion; lead with the chill zone gif and the damage-vs-control framing.
